posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
• 2010 Edmond Vatan Sancerre Clos la Néore - France, Loire Valley, Upper Loire, Sancerre (12/19/2021)
    Open in bottle for 2.5hrs…2 people thought if was corked, not on the nose, and not the musty kind, but on the palate and finish…seemed muted and “off” I guess? I, as well as others, couldn’t for the life of us see it! Maybe less expressive than the last bottle I had 3yrs ago…but this was ALL crazy crushed geology…with zingy citrus cut of lemons and limes…sour green apple skin…exotics of quince paste, lanolin oil, fresh flowers and herbs. LOVE the chalky, salty crushed rocks in these wines…makes it so unique and special when integrated with the bright electric citrus. Maybe not the most exciting finish…mellow…some wet wool and sour apple…but I still dug it! A fascinating wine to drink throughout the night, as there is always something else you pick out with each small pour. And more pours for me, as the ‘corked’ guys were scaring people away! Such a fun drink! (95 pts.)

Posted from CellarTracker

600×800 141 KB

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked: Anyone have the 09 recently? It was an alc hot mess last time I had it? I didn’t want to risk it on the fellas…so I brought the ‘corked’ one instead!

posts.name: Faryan Amir-Ghassemi
posts.cooked: This wine is a bomb. The 09 is purportedly a legend in the making. Thanks for the note

posts.name: Ed Gonzales
posts.cooked: this is one of the best corked bottle ever.

posts.name: Ryan Wihera
posts.cooked: I had this wine at a wonderful wine bar in Dijon a few years ago and I remember the “corked” sensation as well, but, after some time open, the wine opened up beautifully as you mentioned! I find that these styles of Sancerre don’t want to be chilled very much at all, and sometimes show way better on day 2 - with all the quince and lanolin stuff coming out stronger.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
this is one of the best corked bottle ever.

Sucks to be such a highly trained super taster like you and Chris!

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked:
Can you guys tell me how well these wines age?
At what age (post-vintage) do you guys prefer to drink these?
TIA

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Can you guys tell me how well these wines age?
At what age (post-vintage) do you guys prefer to drink these?
TIA

In general, age 20+, but I have had some premox issues with '10, '11 and '13, so…

posts.name: David_Bloch
posts.cooked: The 2007 is also splendid and this note on the 2010 really reflects all of the nuances found in the slightly older wine. It is undoubtedly a towering wine by any measure. The layers of complexity unfold for minutes in the mouth and the 2007 had a very driven finish as well.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Can you guys tell me how well these wines age?
At what age (post-vintage) do you guys prefer to drink these?
TIA

I was generally drinking these wines relatively young - went through about a case during this Covid era - but a recent 2005 that I had with Todd and the boys made me totally rethink things here. That wine had a remarkable complexity to it that really spoke of the benefits of maturation. It was my WOTW in a really stout line-up of killer reds and vintage champagnes.

'07 was Edmond’s last vintage I think

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: I’ve never had a Vatan. Is it truly unique and revelatory, like Rougeard? Or Yquem? Or are there other producers of similar style and quality, and Vatan has just been anointed by the hipster somms? Not sure I’m ever going to find out, given current pricing.
